behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the backoff rule itself (pure calculation over attempt count and max cap). No module handoff, OS/network, or subprocess is required. Higher levels were rejected because behavior text is only about timing formula: "doubles ... up to a configured maximum."
behaviors.test_description: Test a pure backoff function/object with deterministic inputs: attempts 1..N and configured cap. Assert exponential sequence and cap enforcement (e.g., 100,200,400,800,800). Include edge cases like zero/negative attempts if supported.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: Real failure is at a local module boundary: HTTP response classification + retry/backoff policy wiring. A unit test of only the calculator can miss miswired status/header parsing. System was rejected because behavior does not require real network conditions; in-process stub responses can reproduce the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry middleware with a stub transport returning a rate-limit response (e.g., 429 plus retry metadata if used). Assert it is classified as retriable-quota and that computed delay passed to scheduler matches policy.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Needs coordination of timer/wait logic, retry executor, and request replay/response mapping. This is multi-module in-process behavior. System was rejected because real HTTP is not required by the behavior text; a stub transport plus controlled clock can expose resend bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake clock + stub transport that returns rate-limited once then success. Call `call()` once; advance clock by expected delay; assert transport was invoked twice with equivalent original request payload and final resolved value is the success result.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears when retry-attempt counting and terminal error mapping interact. Unit-testing error class alone is insufficient. System rejected: no real OS/network needed; deterministic stubbed repeated 429s reproduce this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Configure low `maxRetries`; stub transport always returns rate-limit response. Await `call()` rejection and assert instance/type is `QuotaExhaustedError` (and not generic error), with expected metadata such as attempts if defined.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly targets full in-process chain across retry controller + backoff + quota state. This is broader than unit. System considered but rejected because text does not require real external infrastructure; fake clock/transport can prove bounded termination and catch infinite-loop logic bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Run `call()` with stubbed always-rate-limited responses, bounded max retries, and fake timers. Assert completion (rejection) occurs within bounded virtual time, attempt count equals policy limit, and no additional retries are scheduled after terminal error.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is shared-state wiring under concurrency across callers/modules. Unit tests of quota arithmetic alone can miss per-call-instance isolation bugs. System rejected because same-process async concurrency can be reproduced in-process without real network/OS primitives.
behaviors.test_description: Create one client/account context and fire concurrent `call()` invocations via promises. Use stub transport/quota responses to force contention. Assert aggregate allowed attempts respect one shared bucket and that callers observe throttling based on global, not per-caller, quota state.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Requires queueing + scheduler + dispatch module interaction; ordering bugs emerge at handoff boundaries. Not Unit because single-function logic cannot validate end-to-end enqueue/dequeue under async waits. System rejected: real network/subprocess is unnecessary; stubbed dispatcher captures order violations.
behaviors.test_description: Enqueue multiple calls rapidly while quota/wait state blocks immediate send. Release capacity stepwise (via fake clock or controlled quota responses). Assert actual dispatch order matches submission order (FIFO) and no later request overtakes earlier ones.
